conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To get started, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have one child – my son Alex. He's 17 and lives at home with me here in Leeds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 17, I need to ask you a bit more about his current situation.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, college courses, or an apprenticeship)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in education at the moment. He finished school but hasn't gone back into any courses or training yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I need to ask a couple more questions about Alex's current situation.

Is Alex currently in paid work, and if so, how many hours per week does he work?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't really work. He does a bit of odd jobs here and there but nothing regular or substantial.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just one more question to complete my assessment:

Is Alex claiming any benefits in his own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not claiming any benefits himself.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit based on what you've told me.

Based on the official guidance, I now have enough information to make a determination about your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

Here's why: Since Alex is 17 (over 16), Child Benefit eligibility depends on him being in approved education or training. The rules state that you can continue to receive Child Benefit for children aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training (such as A-levels, college courses, or certain training programs).

As Alex has finished school and is not currently in any approved education or training, you don't meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

**However, there is some good news:** If Alex were to register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, you could potentially claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks after he left education. Also, if he decides to return to approved education or training in the future, you could become eligible again at that point.

If you have any questions about this decision or if Alex's circumstances change, please feel free to ask.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which means he is over 16. Under the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for children under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you can still qualify if the child is under 20 AND they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex has finished school and is not currently in any approved education, training, or apprenticeship. He is not working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment, and he is not claiming benefits in his own right. While these factors don't disqualify him, the key issue is that he is over 16 and not in approved education or training. According to the rules, Child Benefit eligibility ends when a child over 16 is no longer in approved education or training, unless they have registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which provides up to 20 weeks of continued benefit). There is no indication that Alex has registered with such services. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives at home in Leeds. Alex is not currently in approved education or training, which is the critical requirement for Child Benefit eligibility for children over 16. As Alex does not meet this requirement and there is no indication of registration with careers services or armed forces, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

There is no indication that Alex has registered with such services. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Alex is not currently in approved education or training, which is the critical requirement for Child Benefit eligibility for children over 16. As Alex does not meet this requirement and there is no indication of registration with careers services or armed forces, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
